county_metadata.record_losses: Some early records damaged during Black Hawk War evacuations in the 1860s-1870s. Most records reconstructed.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1865 from Sanpete County. Named for the Sevier River. The Black Hawk War (1865-1872) caused temporary abandonment of many Sevier County settlements. Settlers returned and rebuilt in the early 1870s.
county_metadata.research_tips: Sevier County Recorder in Richfield holds deed records from 1865, though some early records were damaged during Black Hawk War evacuations when settlements were temporarily abandoned (1866-1871). LDS ward records for Richfield, Salina, Monroe, Elsinore, and Glenwood document settlers both before and after the war abandonment. The Sevier Valley was the epicenter of the Black Hawk War — militia records, casualty lists, and government correspondence at the Utah State Archives are essential for understanding family disruptions. Many families that evacuated returned to the same land — re-settlement land records at the county recorder's office document this process. The Big Rock Candy Mountain area attracted prospectors. Check Sanpete County for pre-1865 family records. Sevier County DUP museums in Richfield and Salina have pioneer journals documenting the evacuation and return. The Sevier Valley Ancestor newspaper published genealogical queries.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Grantor/grantee deed books, deeds of trust, and mortgages from 1865 to present. County Recorder maintains index books.

Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints ward and branch records for Sevier County communities. Includes blessing records (births), baptisms, confirmations, ordinations, marriages, membership records, and death notices. Functions as de facto vital records for most of Utah's history.
Marriage licenses and certificates from 1865. State registration from 1887 (territorial), comprehensive from 1905.

Federal population schedules for Sevier County. Utah Territory enumerated from 1850. Agricultural, manufacturing, and mortality schedules provide additional family details. 1880 schedule notes polygamous households.
Historical newspapers from Sevier County communities. Utah Digital Newspapers (J. Willard Marriott Library, U of U) has extensive digitized Utah papers. Chronicling America has additional titles.

Cemetery inscriptions, burial records, and sexton records for Sevier County. BillionGraves (headquartered in Utah) has particularly strong coverage of Utah cemeteries. DAR and DUP transcriptions also available.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major record losses. Records intact from formation.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1854 from the extinct Green River County. Summit County includes Park City, one of Utah's most famous mining towns. The county was on the route of the transcontinental railroad. Park City's silver mines drew thousands of immigrant workers.
county_metadata.research_tips: Summit County Recorder in Coalville holds deed records from 1854. Park City mining records are exceptional — the Ontario, Daly West, and Silver King mines employed thousands and generated detailed employment records, payroll books, and accident reports. Park City Museum has mining company records and family files. The immigrant workforce included Cornish, Irish, Italian, Swedish, Finnish, and Chinese miners — ethnic fraternal lodge records (AOH, IOOF, Knights of Pythias) are at the Park City Museum and Utah State Archives. LDS ward records for Coalville, Henefer, and Kamas cover the agricultural communities. The Union Pacific Railroad reached Coalville and Echo in the 1860s — UP records at Omaha. Park City was a non-Mormon town unusual in 19th-century Utah; saloon licenses, mining claim records, and civil court records are more useful than church records for Park City families. The 1898 Park City fire destroyed parts of Main Street — fire insurance records at the Utah State Archives.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major record losses. Records well-preserved from territorial organization.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1850 as one of the original Utah Territory counties. Tooele County includes the Great Salt Lake Desert and the Bonneville Salt Flats. Settled by Mormon pioneers in 1849. Significant military presence with Tooele Army Depot and Dugway Proving Ground.
county_metadata.research_tips: Tooele County Recorder has deed records from 1850. LDS ward records for Tooele (organized 1849), Grantsville, Stockton, and Ophir are on FamilySearch. The Tooele Pioneer Museum and DUP Museum have family files. Mining in Ophir and Stockton drew non-Mormon workers in the 1870s-1880s — mining district records and camp records document a diverse population. The International Smelting and Refining Company at Tooele employed hundreds; company records may be accessible through the Utah State Archives. Tooele Army Depot (1942+) and Dugway Proving Ground (1942+) brought military families — civilian employment records through OPM and military records at NARA. The Goshute Indian Reservation (Skull Valley) is in Tooele County — BIA records at NARA Denver. The Lincoln Highway and the Pony Express route crossed Tooele County; station records at the Utah State Archives. Wendover AAF trained Enola Gay crew — military records at NARA.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major record losses. Settlement restricted until reservation opening.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1880 from Wasatch County. Named for the Ute people. Most of the county was part of the Uintah Reservation until 1905. Vernal was settled in the 1870s on the margin of the reservation. The county is known for oil, gas, and gilsonite mining.
county_metadata.research_tips: Uintah County Recorder in Vernal has deed records from 1880. Bureau of Indian Affairs records for the Uintah Agency at NARA Denver are essential for Ute genealogy — allotment records, agency census rolls, and annuity payment records. The 1905 reservation opening generated extensive GLO homestead records at BLM. LDS ward records for Vernal (organized 1879), Ashley, Naples, and Jensen on FamilySearch. The Uintah County Library Regional History Center in Vernal has an outstanding collection of pioneer journals, photographs, and family histories. Gilsonite mining (unique to the Uintah Basin) employed workers documented in mine company records at the Utah State Archives. The Daughters of Utah Pioneers museum in Vernal has pioneer artifacts. Outlaw Trail history — Butch Cassidy connections documented in court records. Oil and gas lease records at the Utah Division of Oil, Gas and Mining identify landowners. Check Wasatch County for pre-1880 records.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major record losses. Excellent record preservation from earliest settlement.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1850 as one of the original Utah Territory counties. Utah County was the site of the first Mormon settlement south of Salt Lake Valley (1849). Provo is home to Brigham Young University. Utah Valley has been one of the most populous areas in the state since settlement.
county_metadata.research_tips: Utah County Recorder in Provo has deed records from 1849. Brigham Young University's L. Tom Perry Special Collections hold extensive Utah Valley pioneer journals, family histories, and church records — one of the premier genealogical research libraries in the western US. LDS ward records for Provo (1849), Springville, Spanish Fork, Payson, Lehi, American Fork, and Pleasant Grove are extensive and essential on FamilySearch. The Utah Valley Regional Family History Center (a BYU/FamilySearch facility) provides free access to major genealogical databases. Utah County had conflicts with Ute and Timpanogos people in the early 1850s (Walker War, 1853-54) — territorial militia records at the Utah State Archives. The D&RGW Railroad and UP railroad records document transportation workers. Geneva Steel records (1942-2001) at the Utah State Archives document wartime and postwar industrial workers. Provo City Library has local history collections.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major record losses. Records well-preserved from formation.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1862 from Utah, Summit, and the extinct Green River counties. Wasatch County occupies the Heber Valley east of the Wasatch Mountains. Settled by Mormon pioneers in the late 1850s-early 1860s.
county_metadata.research_tips: Wasatch County Recorder in Heber City holds deed records from 1862. LDS ward records for Heber (organized 1859), Midway, Charleston, and Daniel are the primary genealogical sources on FamilySearch. Midway was settled by Swiss and German Mormon converts — distinctive cultural records including German-language LDS ward records on FamilySearch. The Heber Valley Railroad (Heber Creeper) records document transportation history. Wasatch County was a parent to Duchesne County (1914) — check both counties for families that moved east into the Uintah Basin. The Wasatch County DUP Museum and Heber Valley Historical Society have pioneer family collections. The Provo River diversion and irrigation records at the State Engineer's Office document water rights holders. Deer Creek Reservoir (1941) displaced some families — Bureau of Reclamation records at NARA Denver. Sheep ranching was significant; woolgrowers' association records identify herders including Basque immigrants.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major record losses. Excellent preservation despite southern Utah's harsh climate.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1852. Washington County encompasses Utah's 'Dixie' — Brigham Young sent settlers to grow cotton in the warm climate. St. George was founded in 1861. The St. George Temple (1877) was the first completed LDS temple in Utah.
county_metadata.research_tips: Washington County Recorder in St. George has deed records from 1852. The LDS Cotton Mission (1857-1870s) is extensively documented in church records — mission journals at the LDS Church History Library name every called family. St. George Temple records (1877+) on FamilySearch document the earliest temple ordinances in Utah. LDS ward records for St. George, Santa Clara, Toquerville, Springdale, Leeds, and Virgin are extensive. Washington County's Dixie colonists included Swiss, German, and Southern converts tasked with growing cotton. The Brigham Young Winter Home in St. George preserves correspondence. Washington County was a parent to Kane County (1864). Southern Paiute bands lived in Washington County — the Shivwits Reservation records at BIA/NARA Denver. Dixie State University (formerly College) Archives have local history materials. The Mountain Meadows Massacre (1857) site is in present-day Washington County — extensive records at the Utah State Archives and LDS Church History Library.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Grantor/grantee deed books, deeds of trust, and mortgages from 1852 to present. County Recorder maintains index books.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major record losses. Records sparse due to very small population.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1892 from Piute County. Wayne County is extremely remote and sparsely populated. Includes Capitol Reef National Park. Settled by Mormon pioneers in the 1870s-1880s.
county_metadata.research_tips: Wayne County Recorder in Loa has deed records from 1892. With a population historically under 3,000, Wayne County records are thin but well-preserved. LDS ward records for Loa, Bicknell, Torrey, Hanksville, and Caineville are the primary sources on FamilySearch. Capitol Reef National Park (established 1937 as a monument, 1971 as a park) encompasses former homesteads — NPS records at NARA document families who lived within park boundaries, particularly the Fruita community. Hanksville was one of Utah's most isolated communities and a waypoint on the Outlaw Trail. Check Piute County for pre-1892 records. The Wayne County DUP collection includes pioneer journals documenting the extreme isolation of these communities. Ranching and limited mining are documented in BLM and state records. Wayne Wonderland newspaper clippings at the Utah State Archives document families in detail due to the small community size.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Grantor/grantee deed books, deeds of trust, and mortgages from 1892 to present. County Recorder maintains index books.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major record losses. Excellent record preservation — Ogden was a major railroad hub.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1850 as one of the original Utah Territory counties. Ogden was Utah's railroad hub and second-largest city. The transcontinental railroad junction was at Ogden. More ethnically and religiously diverse than most Utah communities due to railroad influence.
county_metadata.research_tips: Weber County Recorder in Ogden has deed records from 1850. Ogden was Utah's railroad crossroads — Union Pacific and Southern Pacific/Central Pacific employment records document thousands of workers. Ogden was notably more diverse than most Utah cities, with significant non-Mormon, Catholic, and immigrant populations. LDS ward records for Ogden, North Ogden, Harrisville, and surrounding communities on FamilySearch. The Weber State University Stewart Library Special Collections hold Ogden business records and family papers. Ogden's 25th Street was a multiethnic commercial district with Greek, Italian, Chinese, Japanese, and African American businesses — city directory research is especially productive. The Union Station museums in Ogden have railroad and local history collections. Fort Douglas (Salt Lake) and the Ogden Arsenal military records at NARA. Defense Depot Ogden (DDOU) civilian employment records. The Ogden Standard-Examiner newspaper (1888+) provides extensive coverage. Japanese community records document the Ogden Buddhist Temple congregation.
